---
title: "sammy_r21 — scRNA-seq QC"
subtitle: "Pre-filter QC across the cohort, to choose clustering thresholds"
format:
  pptx:
    reference-doc: ouhsc_ppt_template.pptx
    # `#` becomes a Section Header slide, `##` a Title-and-Content slide. Without
    # this pandoc infers the level from the headings present, so adding the first
    # `#` would silently demote every `##` to a bullet.
    slide-level: 2
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  # Keep rendering when a cell raises: sample sections are hardcoded, so a section
  # whose h5ad was not staged errors on its own slide instead of taking the whole
  # deck down. Useful while the plots are still being built out. The cost is that a
  # genuinely broken cell yields an error slide and an exit code of 0 — read the
  # slides, not just the exit status.
  error: true
---

```{python}
# | tags: [setup]
from pathlib import Path

import anndata as ad
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from IPython.display import display, Markdown
from matplotlib.colors import BoundaryNorm, ListedColormap

# The reference template is 16:9 at 10 x 5.625in; leave room for a slide title.
FIGSIZE = (9.0, 4.3)

# Condition palette, carried over from oir-analysis so figures across the two
# projects read the same. Teal/orange is a blue-orange axis, which stays separable
# under the common red-green CVDs.
COLORS = {"normal": "#2AABB8", "obese": "#E87D2A"}

# Mito % colour spends its whole range on 30-70 and holds both ends flat. The
# distribution is extremely bottom-heavy (median ~4%), so a full 0-100 scale burns its
# range separating cells that are all equally fine and leaves the manifold a
# near-uniform purple. Below 30 nothing is being decided, and above 70 a cell is dead
# either way — telling 75% from 90% changes no threshold. 30-70 is the band where the
# call actually lives, so that is where the resolution goes.
#
# The top end of 70 is generous, and known to be: it came from per-sample retention
# curves that put the gap between the live and dead populations out at ~72, but the full
# cohort then showed the 60-70 cells sitting in the debris cloud. They are dead; 70 is
# late. Left at 70 on purpose — these bands are a reading aid, nothing downstream reads
# them, and the threshold question is settled (50, fixed, all samples). Moving it changes
# no decision, and by the same evidence 50-60 is probably debris too, so there is no
# principled place to stop.
#
# Note this is the inverse of the trap in this panel's history, not a repeat of it:
# clipping the TOP at 25 collapsed a 25% cell into a 90% one and hid the dead
# population. Clipping at 70 collapses only cells that are already past saving.
#
# Discrete steps, not a smooth ramp: this panel exists to pick a mito threshold, and a
# continuous ramp makes you eyeball where one colour "becomes" another. Steps put the
# candidate cutoffs in the legend itself, so a band you can see is a number you can
# name. Four 10-wide steps on round numbers — the cutoff is going to be one of these.
#
# These edges ARE the cutoffs mito_tally reports, and that correspondence is the point:
# every tick on the colorbar is a row in the annotation, so a band you can see is a
# number you can read off the same slide. Change one and change the other.
MITO_BINS = np.arange(30, 71, 10)  # 30, 40, 50, 60, 70 -> 4 interior bins
MITO_CUTOFFS = list(MITO_BINS)
# Okabe-Ito, not a sampled colormap: a sequential ramp is built so neighbouring steps
# blend, which is precisely wrong when the question is which band a point falls in.
# These are a qualitative set chosen to stay separable under every CVD type. The cost is
# losing the "higher = worse" read — you consult the legend instead of just seeing that
# darker is worse — which is worth it at this few bands with the legend right there.
# Every step also has to clear grey below and red above, so the orange-to-crimson corner
# of Okabe-Ito is unusable here however nice #D55E00 looks.
MITO_STEP_COLORS = ["#0072B2", "#009E73", "#F0E442", "#CC79A7"]  # blue, green, yellow, purple
# Both flat ends need to be distinct from the step colours, or the boundary is invisible
# and a 29% cell looks like a 31% one. Grey under makes the manifold recede — it is not
# the question — and red over reads as dead against steps that never go red.
#
# extend="both" makes BoundaryNorm map the out-of-range regions to the colormap's own
# first and last entries, so grey and red are ordinary colours in the list rather than
# set_under/set_over. That is why the list is len(MITO_BINS)+1: 4 bins plus two ends.
MITO_CMAP = ListedColormap(["#D9D9D9", *MITO_STEP_COLORS, "#C1272D"])
MITO_NORM = BoundaryNorm(MITO_BINS, MITO_CMAP.N, extend="both")

# The cutoff currently under discussion. Not a decision and nothing downstream reads it
# — the report filters nothing. It drives the summary table's "dropped >=N%" column,
# which is the only place discard rates can be compared across samples side by side; the
# per-scatter tallies give the same numbers one sample at a time.
MITO_CANDIDATE = 50

# Every h5ad is staged into this notebook's working directory by the Nextflow
# process, so globbing is the whole input contract — no params to pass or drift.
H5ADS = sorted(Path(".").glob("*.h5ad"))
if not H5ADS:
    raise FileNotFoundError("No .h5ad files staged next to the notebook.")


def load_obs(path):
    """Read one h5ad's obs without pulling X into memory.

    backed="r" leaves the count matrix on disk; obs is all this report needs, and
    the cohort's X would be ~5 GB.
    """
    adata = ad.read_h5ad(path, backed="r")
    obs = adata.obs.copy()
    adata.file.close()
    return obs


def mito_tally(values):
    """Cumulative cells at or above each candidate mito cutoff, as display lines.

    Cumulative rather than per-band because the question is "what do I lose if I cut
    here", and a cut at 40 discards everything above it, not just 40-50.
    """
    lines = []
    for cutoff in MITO_CUTOFFS:
        n = int((values >= cutoff).sum())
        lines.append(f"≥{cutoff}%:{n:>6,} ({n / len(values) * 100:4.1f}%)")
    return lines


def qc_scatter(sample):
    """One slide's figure: this sample's counts-vs-genes scatter, coloured by mito %.

    The joint view is what separates debris from real cells — low-count/low-gene
    points riding high on mitochondrial fraction sit off the main diagonal.
    """
    d = obs[obs["sample"] == sample]
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=FIGSIZE)
    scatter = ax.scatter(
        d["total_counts"], d["n_genes_by_counts"],
        c=d["pct_counts_mt"],
        # Stepped over 30-60, flat outside it — see MITO_BINS.
        cmap=MITO_CMAP, norm=MITO_NORM,
        s=3, alpha=1.0, edgecolors="none", rasterized=True,
    )
    ax.set_xscale("log")
    ax.set_yscale("log")
    ax.set_xlabel("Total counts", fontsize=9)
    ax.set_ylabel("Genes detected", fontsize=9)
    ax.set_title(f"{d['condition'].iloc[0]} · {len(d):,} cells · median {d['pct_counts_mt'].median():.1f}% mito",
                 fontsize=9, loc="left")
    ax.spines[["top", "right"]].set_visible(False)
    ax.tick_params(labelsize=8)

    # One row per colorbar tick: the colour shows where those cells sit, the number says
    # how many there are. Bottom right is the one reliably empty corner — the manifold
    # runs bottom-left to top-right and the debris sits low and left of it. Monospace so
    # the columns line up.
    ax.text(0.99, 0.03, "\n".join(mito_tally(d["pct_counts_mt"])),
            transform=ax.transAxes, ha="right", va="bottom",
            fontsize=7, family="monospace", linespacing=1.5,
            bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.4", facecolor="white",
                      edgecolor="#CCCCCC", linewidth=0.5))

    # Note the colorbar inherits the mappable's alpha: drop the scatter below 1 and the
    # bands render blended toward white. The norm's extend="both" already tells the
    # colorbar to draw the flat ends as arrowheads, so passing extend here too would
    # conflict.
    cbar = fig.colorbar(scatter, ax=ax, fraction=0.03, pad=0.02)
    cbar.set_ticks(MITO_BINS)
    cbar.set_label("Mitochondrial % of counts", fontsize=8)
    cbar.ax.tick_params(labelsize=7)
    fig.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
    plt.close(fig)


# (column, axis label, log x). UMI counts span ~500 to >120,000 with almost all
# cells under 20,000, so a linear axis spends most of the panel on an empty tail.
# The values stay raw either way — only the axis changes.
HIST_METRICS = [
    ("n_genes_by_counts", "Genes per cell", False),
    ("total_counts", "UMI counts per cell", True),
    ("pct_counts_mt", "Mitochondrial % of counts", False),
]


def qc_hists(sample, bins=100):
    """One slide's figure: the three raw-count QC distributions, side by side.

    All three metrics come straight off the raw count matrix — create_adata runs
    calculate_qc_metrics on the unnormalized X with log1p=False, so there is no
    normalized variant of these columns to pick up by mistake.

    y is a cell count rather than a density, so bar heights are the actual number of
    cells. Each panel keeps its own y scale: the three metrics bin differently, and a
    shared axis would flatten two of them to nothing. x scale is per-metric — see
    HIST_METRICS.
    """
    d = obs[obs["sample"] == sample]
    color = COLORS[d["condition"].iloc[0]]
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(9.0, 3.5))

    for ax, (metric, label, log_x) in zip(axes, HIST_METRICS):
        values = d[metric]
        # Log-spaced bins, not just a log axis: linear bins drawn on a log scale
        # render as wildly uneven bar widths and misrepresent the distribution.
        edges = np.logspace(np.log10(values.min()), np.log10(values.max()), bins) if log_x else bins
        ax.hist(values, bins=edges, color=color, edgecolor="none")

        # The median is the anchor the threshold conversation keeps returning to, and
        # a histogram's peak is not it when the tail is this long.
        median = values.median()
        ax.axvline(median, color="black", linewidth=1, linestyle="--")
        shown = f"{median:,.1f}" if metric == "pct_counts_mt" else f"{median:,.0f}"
        ax.annotate(f"median {shown}", xy=(median, 1), xycoords=("data", "axes fraction"),
                    xytext=(3, -8), textcoords="offset points",
                    fontsize=7, va="top", ha="left")

        ax.set_xlabel(label, fontsize=8)
        if log_x:
            ax.set_xscale("log")
        ax.spines[["top", "right"]].set_visible(False)
        ax.tick_params(labelsize=7)
        ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.25, linewidth=0.5)
        ax.set_axisbelow(True)

    axes[0].set_ylabel("Cells", fontsize=8)
    fig.suptitle(f"{sample} · {len(d):,} cells", fontsize=9, x=0.01, ha="left")
    fig.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
    plt.close(fig)


def md_table(df):
    """Render a DataFrame as a markdown table.

    Built by hand rather than via df.style (HTML-only, renders as nothing in pptx)
    or df.to_markdown (needs tabulate, which the container does not carry).
    """
    header = "| " + " | ".join(str(c) for c in df.columns) + " |"
    rule = "| " + " | ".join("---" for _ in df.columns) + " |"
    rows = ["| " + " | ".join(str(v) for v in row) + " |" for row in df.itertuples(index=False)]
    display(Markdown("\n".join([header, rule] + rows)))


obs = pd.concat([load_obs(p) for p in H5ADS], ignore_index=True)
# Order samples by condition then id so the table reads as two blocks.
obs["id_num"] = obs["id"].astype(int)
sample_order = (
    obs[["sample", "condition", "id_num"]]
    .drop_duplicates()
    .sort_values(["condition", "id_num"])["sample"]
    .tolist()
)
sample_condition = obs.drop_duplicates("sample").set_index("sample")["condition"].to_dict()
```

## Per-sample summary

```{python}
def summary_row(label, condition, d):
    """One row of the summary table, for a sample or for the pooled cohort."""
    mt = d["pct_counts_mt"]
    return {
        "sample": label,
        "condition": condition,
        "cells": f"{len(d):,}",
        "median genes": f"{round(d['n_genes_by_counts'].median()):,}",
        "median counts": f"{round(d['total_counts'].median()):,}",
        "median mito %": f"{mt.median():.2f}",
        # The only place discard rates can be compared across samples side by side —
        # the per-scatter tallies give the same numbers one sample at a time. Note
        # cluster.py keeps < MITO_CANDIDATE, so this ">=" is its exact complement;
        # change one and change the other.
        f"dropped ≥{MITO_CANDIDATE}%": f"{(mt >= MITO_CANDIDATE).mean() * 100:.1f}%",
    }


rows = [summary_row(s, sample_condition[s], obs[obs["sample"] == s]) for s in sample_order]
# Cohort total as a final row, not trailing prose: any text after the table gets
# pushed onto a slide of its own, titled with its first sentence fragment.
rows.append(summary_row("cohort", "", obs))
md_table(pd.DataFrame(rows))
```

<!--
Everything below is one hardcoded `# <sample>` block per sample, in samplesheet order
(condition, then ascending id — the same order sample_order produces). Regenerate them
from assets/samplesheet.csv rather than typing ids by hand.

Hardcoding is deliberate, not laziness. Generating the headings in a loop needs
`output: asis`, and under it an inline figure lands between the markdown blocks and
leaves the *next* heading not starting its own line — so pandoc emits a literal
"## sample" as text and drops the image. One cell per sample means plt.show() just
works. The summary table above is the opposite case: it iterates sample_order and so
picks up new samples with no edit here.
-->

# normal_id_1

## Counts vs genes

```{python}
qc_scatter("normal_id_1")
```

## QC distributions

```{python}
qc_hists("normal_id_1")
```

# normal_id_3

## Counts vs genes

```{python}
qc_scatter("normal_id_3")
```

## QC distributions

```{python}
qc_hists("normal_id_3")
```

# normal_id_9

## Counts vs genes

```{python}
qc_scatter("normal_id_9")
```

## QC distributions

```{python}
qc_hists("normal_id_9")
```

# normal_id_17

## Counts vs genes

```{python}
qc_scatter("normal_id_17")
```

## QC distributions

```{python}
qc_hists("normal_id_17")
```

# normal_id_20

## Counts vs genes

```{python}
qc_scatter("normal_id_20")
```

## QC distributions

```{python}
qc_hists("normal_id_20")
```

# normal_id_27

## Counts vs genes

```{python}
qc_scatter("normal_id_27")
```

## QC distributions

```{python}
qc_hists("normal_id_27")
```

# normal_id_33

## Counts vs genes

```{python}
qc_scatter("normal_id_33")
```

## QC distributions

```{python}
qc_hists("normal_id_33")
```

# normal_id_36

## Counts vs genes

```{python}
qc_scatter("normal_id_36")
```

## QC distributions

```{python}
qc_hists("normal_id_36")
```

# normal_id_55

## Counts vs genes

```{python}
qc_scatter("normal_id_55")
```

## QC distributions

```{python}
qc_hists("normal_id_55")
```

# normal_id_61

## Counts vs genes

```{python}
qc_scatter("normal_id_61")
```

## QC distributions

```{python}
qc_hists("normal_id_61")
```

# obese_id_23

## Counts vs genes

```{python}
qc_scatter("obese_id_23")
```

## QC distributions

```{python}
qc_hists("obese_id_23")
```

# obese_id_31

## Counts vs genes

```{python}
qc_scatter("obese_id_31")
```

## QC distributions

```{python}
qc_hists("obese_id_31")
```

# obese_id_57

## Counts vs genes

```{python}
qc_scatter("obese_id_57")
```

## QC distributions

```{python}
qc_hists("obese_id_57")
```

# obese_id_67

## Counts vs genes

```{python}
qc_scatter("obese_id_67")
```

## QC distributions

```{python}
qc_hists("obese_id_67")
```

# obese_id_75

## Counts vs genes

```{python}
qc_scatter("obese_id_75")
```

## QC distributions

```{python}
qc_hists("obese_id_75")
```
